text: Colonic endometriosis is a relatively uncommon presentation of a disease state, yet may be a common part of endometriosis-focused practices.
When complete excision of disease is desired, it may be removed with shave techniques, sutured or stapled disc excisions, or bisegmental resection.
While the case this video comes from was complex and had many elements, the purpose of this video is to demonstrate technique for disc excision of colonic endometriosis with sutured repair.
This case involves a mid-thirties woman with high-grade endometriosis.
As we come into this video, we've already completed a hysterectomy, a left salpingu phorectomy, an extensive resection of endometriosis.
The video shows superficial invasive endometriosis affecting the bowel at the transition area between the rectum and the vagina.
The surgeon identifies another lesion located more proximally in the distal sigmoid colon, which is visible during the procedure.
The video shows a subtle lesion in the sigmoid colon, characterized by a visible kinking of the bowel, which is indicative of invasive bowel wall endometriosis.
The surgeon uses two instruments to palpate the bowel wall, visually identifying a hard nodule within the tissue.
This lesion was not preoperatively diagnosed as it is high enough that it typically will not show up on an MRI and also cannot be felt on a rectovaginal exam.
The surgeon identifies a small lesion in the anterior wall of the bowel, preparing for removal using a disc excision technique followed by double layer suture repair.
The surgeon attempts to advance an EEA sizer underneath the high-positioned bowel lesion, but encounters a stricture that prevents the sizer from passing beneath the lesion.
The surgeon places a suture through the bowel wall lesion and lifts it using the third arm of the surgical instrument, preparing for disc excision.
The surgeon uses a monopolar cutting electrode to fully excise the lesion from the anterior wall of the bowel, carefully cutting around the hard nodule within the tissue.
The surgeon visualizes a dense nodule located in the anterior wall of the sigmoid colon, observing its motion in response to the manipulation by surgical instruments.
The surgeon uses a monopolar cutting electrode set at fifty to sixty watts to excise the lesion from the anterior wall of the sigmoid colon, following the disc excision technique.
The surgeon uses electrocautery on the bowel, demonstrating a controlled application of the instrument without visible complications.

As general gynecologists we were often taught that to bovie the bowel was absolutely forbidden, but in fact using cut current on the bowel is entirely safe as long as one repairs the defect and in fact general and colorectal surgeons do so frequently.

The surgeon reveals a full defect in the anterior wall of the sigmoid colon after the lesion has been completely excised. The defect appears large but is prepared for closure using suturing techniques.
The surgeon re-opposes the distal and proximal segments of the anterior sigmoid wall, creating a wide lumen with an extended posterior wall.

Notably, such a procedure could also be done with a stapler, but as we noticed, it was difficult to reach a stapler high enough to perform this procedure, thus the performance of the suture technique.
The surgeon begins suturing by placing three O silk sutures at the three and nine o'clock positions of the incision on the bowel.
The surgeon places stay sutures to orient the bowel transversely, ensuring the closure is perpendicular to the lumen of the bowel.
The surgeon uses the left suture in the third arm and the right suture in the assist port to orient the bowel, preparing to initiate a two-layer closure of the bowel defect.
The surgeon initiates the closure by inserting the suture through the mucosa at the three o'clock position and then exits the suture from the mucosa outward, adjacent to the entry point.
The surgeon closes the mucosa using a running layer of 3-0 Vicryl suture, ensuring a continuous closure along the incision line.
The surgeon uses a running suture technique, ensuring the suture enters and exits the mucosa in a box-like pattern to prevent constriction of the final incision.
The surgeon places each suture in front of and exits behind the previous suture in an out-to-in, then in-to-out fashion, ensuring precise alignment for the bowel closure.
The surgeon pulls the suture tight, gradually closing the incision transversely across the bowel.
The surgeon continues placing sutures, progressively closing the incision until the entire length is visibly sealed.

The surgeon methodically closes a large defect using a step-by-step approach, demonstrating how the running suture technique effectively brings the edges together despite the initial size of the defect.
The surgeon adjusts the needle placement from a side-to-side to a more vertical orientation as the incision progressively closes.
The surgeon approaches the opposite edge of the incision, carefully including the corner in the suturing to prevent any potential leakage from the edge.
The surgeon completes the first layer of closure, with the sutured incision visibly sealed along its length.
The surgeon begins creating a second layer of imbricating sutures using interrupted O-silk, adding reinforcement to the closure of the incision.

The surgeon places imbricating sutures that incorporate the bowel serosa and muscularis on both sides of the incision, effectively pulling the tissue over the previously closed mucosa and muscularis layers.
The surgeon places the final sutures, resulting in a reconstructed bowel segment with healthy serosa on both sides and a wide open lumen, resembling a normal piece of bowel.
The surgeon performs a proctoscopy and air leak test on the bowel anastomosis, confirming visually that there is no leakage from the surgical site.
In any case such as this, this test is absolutely required as even with thorough suturing, a small leak may be present and if left unrepaired, fistula or abscess formation is highly likely.
